text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic excision of a para-ovarian cyst, using minimally invasive techniques to carefully remove the cystic structure from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon introduces the secondary trocar under direct vision, carefully avoiding damage to the bowel and other surrounding structures.
The surgeon carefully pushes the bowel away from the operative site to prevent damage during the laparoscopic excision of a para-ovarian cyst.
The uterus is visible with bilateral fallopian tubes adjacent to it.
Gentle pressure is applied to the left fallopian tube, effectively exposing the para-ovarian cyst for further surgical intervention.

The para-ovarian cyst is visible, appearing as a large, simple cyst. A small area is cauterized to prevent bleeding at the entry point. The surgeon makes an incision in the cyst wall using scissors, creating a small nick inside the cyst.

The surgeon drains the fluid from the para-ovarian cyst using a suction cannula, visibly emptying the cyst. The remaining cyst wall is excised with scissors, and hemostasis is achieved using electrocautery.
The surgeon excises the cyst wall, ensuring hemostasis to prevent further bleeding, following the exposure of the para-ovarian cyst.
So thus this procedure is carried out by a minimally invasive technique of laparoscopy which is now the advanced technology.
Thus causing only mild postoperative pain for the patient and early recovery of the patient.
The surgeon secures bleeding and completely excises the cyst using surgical instruments, ensuring the removal of the cyst wall.
